# Extracao: transcription_mp4_11__How_to_Create_a_WHY_Statement

- **Tipo:** TXT

---

You now have the basic components of your Y statement, the contribution and the impact that most stand out for you. We hope you can feel a positive momentum building. The format of a Y statement begins with your contribution and ends with your impact. The structure we recommend using is to insert your contribution so that insert your impact. Before you draft your Y, let's talk about what makes a good Y statement. When putting words to your Y, it's important to keep a few things in mind. First, a Y needs to be simple and clear. The easier it is to understand your Y, the easier it will be for people to identify with and help you live it. Remember, people are not inspired by what you do, people are inspired by why you do it. Keep things like your job title, function or industry out of your Y. There is a place for what you do and that place is in your what's rather than in your Y. Now this can be counterintuitive. We're so used to defining ourselves by what we do that it feels unnatural to leave those things out of our Y. But to include them would actually be very limiting. Our Y is constant. It goes beyond our work or who we are at home. We are who we are wherever we are. Over time, what we do can and likely will change. Our Y, though, remains the same. The next essential characteristic of a good Y statement is for it to be in service of others. Very often people write their Y statements to be about them. Although this is accurate, fulfillment comes from service to others rather than service to self. We often see statements that sound like this. To recognize the value I bring so that I can accomplish more than I thought possible. This Y statement felt right to the person saying it and at the same time it lacks the service component. It's actually a pretty selfish statement if you think about it. Simply changing it to recognize the value people bring so that together we can accomplish more than we thought possible makes this Y a cause worth following. While making this switch will likely inspire others to follow your cause, there's another important key benefit too. What we've discovered is that if you want to be happy, then do something for yourself. On the other hand, if you want to be fulfilled, do something for someone else. When we take what we value most and seek for ourselves and make it available to others so they can share in it too, it is immensely fulfilling. And the Y is all about finding fulfillment. This is how we experience a much deeper satisfaction in life. Okay, moving on to the last aspect of a good Y statement. It needs to be for something. Let me explain. Some people make their Y about something they are against. It's more valuable, however, and more inspiring to use positive language when writing a Y. Make it something you are standing for. For example, instead of writing something like, to fight against evil so that we can live in a peaceful world, explore the positive version. This might be something like, to stand for good so that we can live in a peaceful world. A simple shift gives us something to stand for versus something to fight against. Let's wrap up with an example of a Y statement that meets all of these criteria. We can take Simon's Y. To inspire people to do the things that inspire them so that together we can change our world. First, notice the contribution and impact elements we mentioned before. Next, it's simple and clear. It's free of what? Instant service of others and it's in affirmative language. What's more, the way it's expressed is inspiring to its owner. And that's important too. Boom, good job. Now it's your turn. This involves both of you, the storyteller and the partner working independently to draft Y statements. When you're both ready, type each of your drafts into the course. Then, craft a version together, drawing on the two you completed independently. The goal is to get to a draft that feels right to the storyteller, rather than finding the perfect words. Do a check to make sure it meets the criteria we mentioned before. Simple and clear, free of what? In service to others and in affirmative language. If you find a Y that feels right, even if the words need a little tweaking, you're just where you need to be.
